transcription:
CSE 138 Lecture #2

Why is time useful? Coordination

How do we use clocks?  1) Mark particular instances
in time

2) Measure durations or
intervals of time
Physical
Computers have 2 clocks:  time-of-day clocks
- synchronized between
Monotonic clocks  machines using NTP (Network
Time Protocol)

- only go forward
- bad for timestamping  - bad for measuring durations
particular points in time  intervals of time
- because they can jump
- good for measuring durations  backwards and forwards
intervals of time  (2016 Cloudflare leap
second bug)

Book Rev. Designing Data Intensive
Applications  - Ok (ish) for timestamping
why?  points in time

Any out of sync  consensus
clocks can lead to confusion of ordering

Logical clocks: Only measure the ordering of
events
A→B: A happened before B
- A could have caused B, B could not have caused A
happens before is useful in system design & debugging
↓

- Latency, unbounded in distributed systems, causes all sorts

blocks.text: 2. Measure durations or intervals of time

items.text: **Monotonic clocks**
only go forward
bad for timestamping particular points in time
good for measuring durations / intervals of time
**Time-of-day clocks**
synchronized between machines using NTP (Network Time Protocol)
bad for measuring durations / intervals of time
because they can jump backwards and forwards (2016 Cloudflare leap second bug)
Ok (ish) for timestamping points in time

blocks.text: Book Rev. Designing Data Intensive Applications
Any out of sync clocks can lead to confusion of ordering
Only measure the ordering of events

items.text: A could have caused B, B could not have caused A

blocks.text: happens before is useful in system design & debugging

items.text: X, R: Message Send Event
Q, Z: Message Receive Event
y: Internal Event
$e_1$ and $e_2$ occur on the same process with $e_1$ first
$e_1$ is a message send event and $e_2$ is the corresponding receive event
if there's another event, $e'$, such that $e_1 \to e'$ and $e' \to e_2$, then $e_1 \to e_2$
Violation of causal message delivery: when a Message B caused by Message A arrives before the Message A.
Latency, unbounded in distributed systems, causes all sorts of problems like this

blocks.text: set of assumptions about network behavior

items.text: Synchronous Network: No message takes longer than $\Delta$ units of time (physical) to be delivered (Not for this class)
Asynchronous Network: A network which has no bound for the length of physical time for a message to be delivered
If we know the events we can figure out the state
Given a state, you cannot determine the history of events which lead to this state
